verses.flow: The words of Amos, who was among the shepherds of Tekoa, which he saw concerning Israel in the days of Uzziah king of Judah and in the days of Jeroboam the son of Joash, king of Israel, two years before the earthquake.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Tekoa was a town in Judah approximately 10 miles south of Jerusalem, in the rugged Judean highland. Amos was a noqed (shepherd/sheep-breeder; Strong's H5349), possibly a guild-master of livestock, not a mere laborer — the same term is used of Mesha king of Moab in 2 Kgs 3:4. The earthquake mentioned here was apparently so memorable it is referenced again in Zech 14:5, suggesting a catastrophic seismic event ca. 760 BCE that served as a temporal landmark for a generation.

verses.flow: And he said: "YHWH roars from Zion, and from Jerusalem he utters his voice; and the pastures of the shepherds mourn, and the top of Carmel withers."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The theophanic roar of YHWH from Zion establishes Jerusalem as the true seat of divine authority, not the northern sanctuaries at Bethel or Dan. The withering of Carmel — renowned for its lush fertility — signals that YHWH's judgment overrides the fertility blessings associated with Baal worship prevalent in the north. TYPOLOGY: This image of the divine voice bringing cosmic disruption anticipates the eschatological theophany language of Joel 3:16, which uses nearly identical phrasing, and ultimately the voice of the Son at the final judgment (Heb 12:26).

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH: "For three transgressions of Damascus, and for four, I will not revoke it — because they have threshed Gilead with threshing sledges of iron.
verses.note: HEBREW: The refrain "for three transgressions...and for four" ('al-sheloshah pish'e...ve'al-arba'ah) employs a graded numerical formula common in Wisdom literature (cf. Prov 30:15-31) indicating not a precise count but an overflowing, accumulated fullness of sin — the measure is complete and running over. The word pesha' (Strong's H6588) means a deliberate rebellion or revolt, the strongest Hebrew term for sin as willful transgression. DIVINE_COUNCIL: The formula "I will not revoke it" (lo' ashivennu) likely refers to the irrevocable divine decree issued from the heavenly council — YHWH's judicial sentence, once pronounced before his council, cannot be recalled.

verses.flow: And I will break the gate-bar of Damascus, and cut off the inhabitants from the Valley of Aven, and him who holds the scepter from Beth-eden; and the people of Syria shall go into exile to Kir," says YHWH.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Kir" appears as the place of Aramean origin in Amos 9:7, making this judgment an ironic reversal — exile back to their place of origin. The Valley of Aven ("Valley of Wickedness") may be a deliberate derogatory renaming of the Beqa'a Valley. Beth-eden ("House of Delight/Eden") was a powerful Aramean city-state on the upper Euphrates, known in Assyrian records as Bit-Adini, conquered by Shalmaneser III in 855 BCE.

This is one of the earliest prophetic condemnations of the slave trade, establishing a universal moral order under YHWH that applies even to nations outside the Mosaic covenant.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH: "For three transgressions of Tyre, and for four, I will not revoke it — because they delivered up a whole people to Edom, and did not remember the covenant of brotherhood.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The "covenant of brotherhood" (berit achim) between Tyre and Israel likely refers to the treaty forged between Hiram of Tyre and both David and Solomon (2 Sam 5:11; 1 Kgs 5:1-12). Tyre's violation of this covenant by selling Israelite captives to Edom compounds its guilt with betrayal of formal treaty obligations — a grave offense in ancient Near Eastern diplomacy. THEOLOGICAL: That Tyre is held accountable to a covenant it made with Israel demonstrates that YHWH governs international covenantal ethics, not merely Israel's internal Torah observance.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH: "For three transgressions of Edom, and for four, I will not revoke it — because he pursued his brother with the sword and cast off all pity (rahamim), and his anger tore perpetually, and he kept his wrath forever.
verses.note: HEBREW: The word rahamim (Strong's H7356), translated "pity" or "compassion," derives from the root rechem meaning "womb," conveying the most intimate, instinctive form of mercy — the love of a mother for the child of her womb. Edom's sin is described as the total suppression of this primal compassion toward his own brother Jacob/Israel. THEOLOGICAL: The perpetual and unrelenting nature of Edom's wrath ("his anger tore perpetually, and he kept his wrath forever") contrasts with YHWH's own anger which, though real, is described as momentary in comparison to his enduring hesed (Ps 30:5). TYPOLOGY: The Edom oracles throughout the prophets (Obad; Isa 63; Jer 49) develop into an eschatological type of implacable hostility to God's people, ultimately transformed in NT perspective where "Edom" becomes a cipher for all anti-kingdom opposition.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH: "For three transgressions of the sons of Ammon, and for four, I will not revoke it — because they ripped open the pregnant women of Gilead, that they might enlarge their border.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The atrocity of ripping open pregnant women (biqqa' haroth) was a recognized war crime in the ancient Near East, attested in Assyrian and other records as a tactic of terrorizing populations and destroying the next generation — it was the ultimate negation of future hope. The motive given — territorial expansion — makes this a prophetic indictment of land-greed as the driving force behind dehumanizing violence. THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's condemnation of Ammon for this act reveals that universal human rights rooted in the imago Dei are enforced by the divine King even among nations who possess no written Torah.

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH: "For three transgressions of Moab, and for four, I will not turn it back — because he burned the bones of the king of Edom to lime.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The burning of the bones of the king of Edom to lime (Hebrew: sid) was an act of extreme desecration in the ancient Near East, denying the dead proper burial and rest. This violated widely-held ANE conventions about the sanctity of royal remains. Notably, Moab's crime was against Edom — a non-Israelite nation — demonstrating that YHWH's moral governance extends to all peoples, not Israel alone. (Gen 9:6; Rom 2:14-15)

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH: "For three transgressions of Judah, and for four, I will not turn it back — because they have rejected the law (torah) of YHWH, and his statutes they have not kept, and their lies have led them astray, those after which their fathers walked.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Torah" (H8451) denotes instruction or law, specifically YHWH's revealed teaching given through Moses. "Statutes" (chuqqim, H2706) are the engraved, fixed divine ordinances. Judah's sin is not ignorance but willful rejection — a heightened culpability. THEOLOGICAL: That Judah receives the same structural oracle-formula as the pagan nations is striking; possession of the Torah increases, rather than diminishes, accountability before YHWH. (Luke 12:48; Rom 2:17-24)

verses.flow: Thus says YHWH: "For three transgressions of Israel, and for four, I will not turn it back — because they sell the righteous for silver, and the needy for a pair of sandals.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The indictment against Israel is the longest and most detailed of all eight oracles, occupying verses 6-16. Selling the righteous (tsaddiq) for silver echoes Joseph's betrayal (Gen 37:28) and anticipates the betrayal of the Righteous One for thirty pieces of silver (Matt 26:15; Acts 7:52). TYPOLOGY: The "righteous" sold for silver foreshadows the Messiah, the supremely Righteous One, sold by Judas. (Zech 11:12-13; Matt 27:9)

verses.note: HEBREW: "Anawim" (H6041) — the afflicted, humble, and oppressed poor — is a theologically loaded term in the Psalter and prophets, often describing those who have no human advocate and thus depend entirely upon YHWH. (Ps 9:12; 37:11; Matt 5:5) THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "profane my holy name" (chalal, H2490, + shem qodshi) shows that social injustice is not merely an ethical failure but an act of sacrilege — a desecration of YHWH's very character revealed in Israel. (Lev 22:2; Ezek 36:20-23)

verses.flow: And I raised up some of your sons as prophets, and some of your young men as Nazirites (nazir). Is it not indeed so, O sons of Israel?" declares YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's raising up of prophets and Nazirites (nazir, H5139 — one consecrated or separated) was an act of covenantal grace and ongoing divine provision within Israel. The Nazirite vow (Num 6:1-21) was a living sign of consecration to YHWH within the community. That Israel silenced these very gifts reveals a rejection of divine governance itself. (1 Sam 3:1; Jer 1:5)

Behold, I am pressing you down in your place, as a cart full of sheaves presses down.
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb translated "pressing down" ('uq, H5781) is rare and disputed; some render it "I will make your place narrow" or "I will make you groan." The image of the loaded harvest cart is agricultural and visceral — YHWH's judgment does not merely threaten but crushes with irresistible weight. The harvest imagery also carries eschatological resonance in prophetic literature. (Joel 3:13; Rev 14:15)

verses.flow: And flight shall perish from the swift, and the strong shall not strengthen his power, and the mighty man shall not save his life.
And he who handles the bow shall not stand, and he who is swift of foot shall not save himself, and he who rides the horse shall not save his life.
And he who is courageous among the mighty shall flee away naked in that day," declares YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "That day" (yom hahu') introduces the eschatological horizon of the Day of YHWH, a theme Amos develops extensively (Amos 5:18-20). Even the mightiest warrior flees naked — stripped of all earthly resource and status — before the judgment of YHWH. The total collapse of military might across vv. 14-16 forms a sevenfold pattern, symbolizing complete and inescapable divine retribution. (Amos 5:18; Joel 2:11; Rev 6:15-17)

verses.flow: Hear this word that YHWH has spoken against you, O sons of Israel, against the whole family that I brought up from the land of Egypt, saying:
verses.note: HEBREW: The verb "spoken" (dabar, H1696) frames the prophetic oracle as a formal divine lawsuit. The phrase "whole family" (kol-hamishpachah) emphasizes corporate solidarity — the covenant encompasses all Israel, not merely its leadership. THEOLOGICAL: The charge is addressed to the entire covenant community descended from the Exodus, establishing the covenantal basis for the indictment that follows.

verses.flow: "You only have I known of all the families of the earth; therefore I will visit upon you all your iniquities."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Known" (yada, H3045) is not mere cognitive awareness but intimate covenant relationship — the same word used of marriage and deep personal union (Gen 4:1). Israel's election intensifies rather than removes accountability. TYPOLOGY: This principle of heightened responsibility for those who have received greater revelation finds its fullest expression in the judgment of those who reject the Messiah (Luke 12:48; Matt 11:20-24).

verses.flow: "If a ram's horn (shofar) is sounded in a city, do the people not tremble? If calamity befalls a city, has not YHWH done it?"
verses.note: HEBREW: "Ram's horn" translates shofar (H7782), the instrument of alarm and assembly used throughout Israel's warfare and liturgy. "Calamity" (ra'ah, H7451) can mean evil, disaster, or adversity — its range includes both moral evil and consequential judgment. THEOLOGICAL: The rhetorical logic asserts YHWH's sovereign governance over all historical catastrophe, a cornerstone of Amos's theology of divine judgment.

verses.flow: "For surely the Lord (Adonai) YHWH does nothing without revealing His secret counsel (sod) to His servants the prophets."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Secret counsel" (sod, H5475) denotes the intimate deliberative council — the same term used in Ps 89:7 and Jer 23:18, 22 for the divine assembly where YHWH's decrees are issued. DIVINE_COUNCIL: Amos here asserts that the prophets are those who have been admitted into the sod of YHWH — they are privy to the decisions of the heavenly council, functioning as YHWH's heralds to earth (cf. 1 Kgs 22:19-22; Jer 23:18). This verse is foundational for understanding the prophetic office in its cosmic dimension.

verses.note: HISTORICAL: Ashdod was a major Philistine city-state; Egypt, a perennial rival and sometime ally of Israel. verses.flow: "For on the day I visit upon Israel its transgressions, I will visit upon the altars of Bethel; and the horns of the altar shall be cut off and fall to the ground."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: Bethel (lit. "house of God") was the premier royal sanctuary of the northern kingdom, established by Jeroboam I with golden calves as an alternative to Jerusalem worship (1 Kgs 12:28-29). "Horns of the altar" were projections at each corner considered most sacred — to cut them off was to utterly desecrate and nullify the altar's atoning function. TYPOLOGY: The destruction of these false cult sites anticipates the ultimate cleansing of corrupt worship, fulfilled eschatologically in the Messiah who alone is the true altar and sacrifice (Heb 13:10).

verses.flow: "And I will strike down the winter house together with the summer house, and the houses of ivory shall perish, and the great houses shall come to an end," declares YHWH.
verses.note: HISTORICAL: "Houses of ivory" (battei hashen) are attested archaeologically — ivory inlay panels and furnishings were luxury items of the Israelite elite, confirmed by the Samaria Ivories discovered in excavations of Omride palace quarters. "Winter house" and "summer house" indicate the extreme opulence of the ruling class. THEOLOGICAL: The destruction of luxury estates embodies YHWH's judgment against the social injustice funded by the oppression of the poor — a theme Amos will develop through the remainder of the book.

verses.flow: Hear this word, you cows of Bashan, who are on the mountain of Samaria, who oppress (ashaq) the poor, who crush the needy, who say to their lords, "Bring, that we may drink!"
verses.note: HEBREW: "Cows of Bashan" (parot ha-Bashan) is a biting irony — Bashan was renowned for its fat, well-fed cattle (Deut 32:14; Ezek 39:18). The prophet applies this image to the wealthy women of Samaria who lived in ease while exploiting the poor. "Oppress" (ashaq, Strong's H6231) denotes crushing economic oppression, often involving the withholding of wages or legal rights. THEOLOGICAL: The accusation that these women commanded their husbands ("lords," adoneihem) to serve their appetites inverts the household order and places social exploitation at the feet of the elite class — a direct covenant violation (Lev 19:13).

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: YHWH swears "by His holiness" (b'qodsho) — one of the most absolute oath formulas in the prophetic corpus, parallel to His swearing "by Himself" (Gen 22:16; Amos 6:8). What is sworn by His essential nature cannot be revoked. HISTORICAL: The imagery of hooks and fishhooks describes deportation practices attested in Assyrian reliefs, where captives were literally led by hooks through the lips or nose — a fitting fulfillment given the Assyrian exile of the Northern Kingdom in 722 B.C. (2 Kgs 17:6).

verses.flow: "Come to Bethel, and transgress; to Gilgal, multiply transgression; and bring your sacrifices every morning, your tithes every three days;
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This passage is biting prophetic irony (sarcasm) — YHWH ironically invites Israel to Bethel and Gilgal to "multiply transgression" (pish'u). Both sites were loaded with covenantal history: Gilgal was where Israel first camped after crossing the Jordan (Josh 4:19); Bethel was where Jacob met God (Gen 28:19). By Amos's day both had become centers of syncretistic, illegitimate worship (1 Kgs 12:29). Their religious zeal was real, but it was directed at corrupted institutions, making devotion itself an act of rebellion. TYPOLOGY: This pattern — outward religious observance without justice or covenant faithfulness — is condemned by Christ in Matt 23:23 and echoes throughout the NT prophetic tradition.

verses.flow: offer a thank offering (todah) of that which is leavened, and proclaim freewill offerings (nedavot), publish them; for so you love to do, O children of Israel," declares the Lord YHWH.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Thank offering" (todah, Strong's H8426) derives from the root yadah, meaning to praise or acknowledge. Leavened bread was explicitly forbidden in most offerings (Lev 2:11), making a leavened todah a ritual violation. "Freewill offerings" (nedavot, Strong's H5071) were voluntary gifts, but here they are performed as public spectacle. THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "for so you love to do" (ki ken ahavtem) is devastatingly ironic — their worship is self-serving performance, not YHWH-directed devotion. True worship and social justice cannot be separated in the prophetic vision (Isa 1:11-17; Mic 6:6-8).

verses.flow: "And I also gave you cleanness of teeth in all your cities, and lack of bread in all your places, yet you did not return to Me," declares YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The refrain "yet you did not return to Me" (v'lo-shavtem adai) appears five times in this chapter (vv. 6, 8, 9, 10, 11), creating a devastating liturgical structure of judgment. Each escalating disaster was a covenant discipline designed to provoke repentance (teshuvah), but Israel refused. HISTORICAL: "Cleanness of teeth" is a vivid idiom for famine — teeth are clean because there is no food to dirty them. This was a covenant curse stipulated in advance (Lev 26:26; Deut 28:48).

verses.note: HISTORICAL: The comparison to the overthrow of Sodom and Gomorrah (Gen 19:24-25) invokes the most catastrophic divine judgment in Israel's memory. The phrase "brand plucked from the burning" (ud mutzal mi'sreifah) suggests a remnant that barely survived — Israel has been to the edge of annihilation and still has not repented. THEOLOGICAL: This image of a "brand plucked from the burning" is later applied to Joshua the high priest in Zech 3:2, suggesting a typological trajectory of remnant rescue that culminates in redemptive restoration.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: "Prepare to meet your God" (hikon liqrat Elohecha) is among the most solemn summons in the Old Testament. The verb hikon (from kun, Strong's H3559) means to make oneself firm, established, ready — used of ceremonial preparation (Exod 19:11, 15). The meeting envisioned here is not a salvation encounter but a judicial one — Israel is being summoned before the divine court. TYPOLOGY: The language anticipates the eschatological Day of YHWH, in which all nations and peoples will face the divine tribunal (Rev 6:17; Mal 3:2).

verses.flow: For behold, He who forms the mountains and creates the wind (ruach), and declares to man what is His thought, who makes the dawn into darkness, and treads upon the heights of the earth — YHWH, the God of hosts (Elohei Tseva'ot), is His name.
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The closing doxology identifies YHWH as "God of hosts" (Elohei Tseva'ot, Strong's H6635 for tsava) — the commander of the heavenly armies and the divine council. This title situates YHWH's judgment not merely as a personal act but as the decree of the supreme sovereign over all cosmic and earthly powers. HEBREW: "Wind" or "spirit" (ruach, Strong's H7307) is deliberately ambiguous — YHWH creates both the physical wind and, by extension, the animating breath of all creatures. "Declares to man what is His thought" (maggid l'adam mah-sicho) emphasizes YHWH's condescension in communicating — the prophet himself is the instrument of that divine self-disclosure. STRUCTURE: This is the first of three doxological hymns in Amos (cf. 5:8-9; 9:5-6), each functioning as a cosmic affirmation of YHWH's unrivaled sovereignty to anchor the surrounding oracles of judgment.

verses.note: HEBREW: The term "lamentation" here is qinah (H7015), a specific genre of funeral dirge with a distinctive 3+2 meter in Hebrew poetry. Amos adopts this genre to declare Israel's fate as already accomplished — the prophetic perfect tense renders "she has fallen" as a done deal before it occurs historically. STRUCTURE: This opening sets the elegiac tone for the entire chapter.

The image anticipates NT imagery of the Church as the Bride (Rev 19:7) and the profound cost of covenant unfaithfulness. THEOLOGICAL: The absence of a "raiser" echoes the covenant curse language of Leviticus 26.

verses.flow: But let justice (mishpat) roll down like waters, and righteousness (tsedaqah) like an ever-flowing stream.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Verse 24 is among the most cited verses in biblical prophetic ethics — "let justice roll down like waters." The imagery of a nahal eithan (perennial stream, never going dry) contrasts with the intermittent wadis of the Negev that flood briefly and vanish. TYPOLOGY: Martin Luther King Jr. famously quoted this verse, but its original context demands justice as a permanent structural reality, not a seasonal occurrence, grounded in YHWH's own covenantal character.

verses.flow: Did you bring to me sacrifices and offerings during the forty years in the wilderness, O house of Israel?
verses.note: HISTORICAL: This verse raises the question of the nature of wilderness worship — some scholars see it as a rhetorical question expecting "no" (you did not offer properly), while others read it as irony. The forty years in the wilderness forms the normative baseline for covenant relationship (cf. Jer 7:22-23; Hos 2:14-15). THEOLOGICAL: The point is that YHWH sought loyalty and obedience, not the mechanical offering of sacrifices divorced from the heart.

The MT preserves the consonants of these names but vocalizes them with the vowels of shiqquts (abomination) — a scribal polemical device. HISTORICAL: Stephen quotes this verse in Acts 7:42-43 (following the LXX rendering as "Moloch" and "Rephan"), applying it to Israel's idolatry as the reason for exile, demonstrating that Amos's indictment reached into the NT era.

verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The command "Silence! For we must not mention the name of YHWH" is one of the most theologically dense phrases in Amos. In the context of death and devastation so total that the survivors fear even invoking YHWH's name — whether from superstitious terror, shame, or a sense that further divine attention would be catastrophic — it communicates the utter dissolution of covenant relationship. HISTORICAL: The burning of the dead is unusual in Israelite practice, suggesting conditions of plague or mass death so severe that normal burial is impossible.

THEOLOGICAL: YHWH as the God of hosts (tseva'ot) who "raises up" (meqim) a foreign nation is a potent declaration of his sovereignty over all kingdoms of the earth.

verses.flow: Then Amos answered and said to Amaziah, "I was no prophet (nabi), nor was I the son of a prophet; but I was a herdsman (boqer) and a dresser of sycamore trees.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: Amos's declaration — "I was no prophet, nor was I the son of a prophet" — is one of the most striking claims of divine call in the Hebrew canon. He explicitly disclaims membership in the professional prophetic guilds (benei ha-nevi'im), insisting his authority derives solely from YHWH's sovereign commission, not institutional credentials. TYPOLOGY: This pattern of the called outsider — shepherd, farmer, fisherman — who speaks divine truth against established religious power prefigures the ministry of Jesus (Matt 4:18-22; John 7:15) and the apostolic pattern of divine calling overriding human qualification (1 Cor 1:26-29).

verses.flow: Therefore thus says YHWH: 'Your wife shall be a harlot in the city, and your sons and your daughters shall fall by the sword, and your land shall be divided by a measuring line, and you yourself shall die in an unclean land, and Israel shall surely go into exile (galah) from its land.'"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The oracle against Amaziah is structured as a precise counter-judgment: because he silenced the word of YHWH, the word of YHWH pronounces judgment on his house, his family, his land, and his life. The threat that he will "die in an unclean land" — i.e., in Assyrian exile — is particularly devastating for a priest for whom ritual purity and access to the sanctuary were central. HISTORICAL: The prophecy of exile (galah, Strong's H1540) for Israel anticipates the Assyrian deportation of 722 B.C. under Sargon II, one of the most thoroughly documented events in both biblical and ANE records, attested in the Sargon II annals from Khorsabad.

verses.flow: Thus my Lord YHWH showed me: and behold, a basket of summer fruit.
verses.note: HEBREW: The "basket of summer fruit" is Hebrew qayits (H7019, ripe/summer fruit). THEOLOGICAL: YHWH employs a wordplay central to the vision: qayits (summer fruit) sounds nearly identical to qets (H7093, "end"), establishing that Israel's ripeness for judgment mirrors fruit ripe for picking. This is a classic prophetic pun (paranomasia) leveraging phonetic similarity to communicate theological reality.

verses.flow: And he said, "What do you see, Amos?" And I said, "A basket of summer fruit." Then YHWH said to me, "The end has come upon my people Israel; I will never again pass by them.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The phrase "I will never again pass by them" (lo' osif 'od 'avor lo) signals the termination of divine forbearance. Earlier in Amos (7:8), YHWH had set a plumb line; now the verdict is irrevocable. TYPOLOGY: The language of YHWH "passing by" echoes the Passover and the theophany to Moses (Exod 34:6), suggesting Israel has exhausted the grace afforded by covenant relationship.

verses.flow: "And it shall come to pass in that day," declares my Lord YHWH, "that I will cause the sun to go down at noon, and I will darken the earth in the broad light of day.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The darkening of the sun at noon anticipates the cosmic sign at the crucifixion of Yeshua the Messiah, when darkness covered the land from the sixth to the ninth hour (Luke 23:44-45). The reversal of creation order (light becoming darkness at midday) signals divine judgment of the most catastrophic kind. THEOLOGICAL: "That day" (yom hahu) is a recurring eschatological marker in the prophets, pointing beyond the immediate Assyrian crisis to ultimate divine reckoning.

verses.flow: "Behold, days are coming," declares my Lord YHWH, "when I will send a famine on the land — not a famine of bread, nor a thirst for water, but of hearing the words (dibre) of YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The "famine of the word of YHWH" represents perhaps the most severe form of divine judgment — not physical destruction but the withdrawal of prophetic revelation. TYPOLOGY: This passage finds partial fulfillment in the 400 years of prophetic silence between Malachi and John the Baptist, and its full resolution in Yeshua as the incarnate Word (dabar/logos, John 1:14), through whom YHWH spoke his final and definitive word (Heb 1:1-2).

verses.flow: "And they shall wander from sea to sea, and from north to east; they shall run to and fro, seeking the word (dabar) of YHWH, but they shall not find it.
verses.note: HEBREW: "Dabar" (H1697) is the comprehensive Hebrew term for "word" or "matter," encompassing divine utterance, prophetic oracle, and covenantal communication. THEOLOGICAL: The frantic geographic searching — "from sea to sea, from north to east" — describes a comprehensive and exhausting quest that yields nothing, the tragic irony being that Israel had despised the word when it was freely given through Amos and his contemporaries.

verses.flow: "Those who swear by the guilt (asham) of Samaria, and say, 'As your god lives, O Dan,' and, 'As the way of Beer-sheba lives' — they shall fall and rise no more."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: "As your god lives, O Dan" is an oath formula invoking the golden calf installed at Dan by Jeroboam I (1 Kgs 12:29), a rival cult center to Jerusalem that embodied Israel's covenant infidelity. THEOLOGICAL: "The way of Beer-sheba" likely refers to a pilgrimage practice at the southernmost cult site, suggesting a syncretic religious tourism that mixed YHWH worship with Canaanite forms. The verdict — "they shall fall and rise no more" — is the final and absolute word of this chapter's judgment oracle.

verses.flow: I saw my Lord standing beside the altar, and He said: "Strike the capitals until the thresholds shake, and shatter them on the heads of all the people; and those who remain of them I will slay with the sword. Not one of them shall flee away; not one of them shall escape."
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The vision of YHWH standing "beside the altar" (not "upon" as in some versions — the Hebrew 'al is ambiguous) signals a divine court judgment scene; the sanctuary itself becomes the site of condemnation. The shaking of thresholds and shattering of capitals echoes the collapse of the cosmos under divine wrath, making escape impossible in every direction (vv. 2-4). TYPOLOGY: The imagery of inescapable divine pursuit anticipates the NT teaching that none can flee the judgment of God (Heb 4:13), while also framing the need for a mediatorial Messiah who absorbs that wrath on behalf of the remnant.

verses.flow: "And if they hide themselves on the top of Carmel, from there I will search them out and take them; and if they conceal themselves from My sight on the floor of the sea, from there I will command the serpent, and it shall bite them."
verses.note: DIVINE_COUNCIL: The "serpent" commanded by YHWH on the floor of the sea recalls the ancient Near Eastern chaos-creature (Leviathan/Tannin) subordinated to YHWH's authority. Far from being an autonomous force, the sea-dragon is here depicted as a servant of the divine council's sovereign Lord. HEBREW: The Hebrew nachash (H5175) refers to a serpent, likely carrying mythological freight from Canaanite cosmology, where sea-monsters represented primordial chaos; Amos subverts this by making the creature an instrument of YHWH's justice.

verses.flow: He who builds His upper chambers in the heavens and has founded His vault upon the earth; He who calls for the waters of the sea and pours them out upon the face of the earth — YHWH is His name.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This doxology (cf. Amos 4:13; 5:8-9) is one of three hymnic fragments in Amos that assert YHWH's cosmic sovereignty. His "upper chambers" (aliyyot, H5944) built in the heavens and His "vault" (aguddah, H92) founded upon the earth depict YHWH as cosmic architect, directly confronting Baalist claims to control of sky and sea. DIVINE_COUNCIL: YHWH's summons of sea-waters echoes the defeat of the sea in Canaanite mythology, here appropriated to assert that what Baal allegedly controlled, YHWH actually does — effortlessly and as sovereign Lord.

verses.flow: "Are you not as the sons of the Cushites to Me, O sons of Israel?" declares YHWH. "Did I not bring up Israel from the land of Egypt, and the Philistines from Caphtor, and Aram from Kir?"
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: This rhetorically explosive verse dismantles Israelite ethnic exceptionalism. YHWH's providential governance extends to the migrations of Cushites, Philistines, and Arameans — traditional enemies — placing them on equal footing as objects of divine action in history. HISTORICAL: Caphtor is generally identified with Crete or the Aegean region, aligning with archaeological and textual evidence for the Sea Peoples' origins. Kir appears in Amos 1:5 as the place of Aram's exile, forming a literary inclusio.

verses.flow: "Behold, the eyes of my Lord YHWH are upon the sinful kingdom, and I will destroy it from the face of the earth — except that I will not utterly destroy the house of Jacob," declares YHWH.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The tension between total destruction and preservation of a remnant is the theological hinge of the entire book. YHWH's judgment is absolute against the "sinful kingdom" as a political entity, but the "house of Jacob" — the covenant people — contains an indestructible remnant held by divine promise. This distinction between institutional Israel and the true remnant runs throughout the prophets (cf. Rom 9:6).

verses.flow: "For behold, I will command, and I will shake the house of Israel among all the nations, as one shakes with a sieve (kevarah), but not a pebble shall fall to the earth."
verses.note: HEBREW: The kevarah (H3531), a sieve or mesh strainer, is the central metaphor: grain passes through (judgment), but the pebbles — here representing the righteous remnant — do not fall to the earth (are not lost). THEOLOGICAL: This sifting image is directly echoed by Jesus in Luke 22:31, where Satan desires to "sift" Peter like wheat, inverting the metaphor — YHWH's sifting preserves the remnant, while the adversary's sifting seeks to destroy. The remnant theology of Amos is foundational to NT ecclesiology.

verses.flow: "In that day I will raise up the fallen booth (sukkah) of David, and wall up its breaches, and raise up its ruins, and rebuild it as in the days of old —"
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: The "fallen booth (sukkah) of David" is the most explicitly messianic text in Amos, directly quoted by James at the Jerusalem Council (Acts 15:16-17) to justify Gentile inclusion in the people of God without full Torah observance. The sukkah (H5521), a temporary shelter, conveys the Davidic dynasty in a state of ruin and collapse — yet YHWH promises its resurrection. THEOLOGICAL: The promise to "raise up" (qum, H6965) the fallen booth anticipates resurrection language; the Davidic covenant, though apparently broken, is not annulled but will be divinely reconstituted in the Messiah (cf. Luke 1:32-33; Rev 3:7).

verses.flow: "— so that they may possess the remnant of Edom and all the nations who are called by My name," declares YHWH who does this.
verses.note: TYPOLOGY: James's citation of this verse in Acts 15:17 (following the LXX, which reads "all mankind" rather than "Edom") provided the hermeneutical key for the Jerusalem Council's decision on Gentile inclusion. The Hebrew 'Edom' and 'adam' (mankind) are textually similar, and the LXX reading may reflect a variant tradition or interpretive tradition. THEOLOGICAL: "Called by My name" (niqra shemi 'alehem) is covenant adoption language — YHWH's name placed upon the nations signals universal covenant extension, the fulfillment of the Abrahamic promise that all families of the earth would be blessed (Gen 12:3).

verses.flow: "Behold, the days are coming," declares YHWH, "when the plowman shall overtake the reaper and the treader of grapes him who sows the seed; and the mountains shall drip sweet wine (asis), and all the hills shall flow with it."
verses.note: HISTORICAL: The images of overlapping agricultural cycles — the plowman overtaking the reaper — depict eschatological abundance so great that harvesting one crop overlaps with planting the next, an inversion of the curses of Lev 26 and Deut 28. HEBREW: Asis (H6071) refers specifically to freshly pressed, sweet grape juice or wine, connoting the richest first-press of the harvest. The "dripping mountains" echo Joel 3:18, forming an intertextual network of eschatological restoration across the Twelve Prophets.

verses.flow: "And I will restore the fortunes (shevut) of My people Israel, and they shall rebuild the ruined cities and inhabit them; and they shall plant vineyards and drink their wine, and they shall make gardens and eat their fruit."
verses.note: HEBREW: The phrase shevut (H7622, also shebut) translated "fortunes" or "captivity" is a covenant-restoration formula appearing across the prophets (Jer 30:3; Ezek 39:25; Zeph 3:20). THEOLOGICAL: YHWH's restoration is comprehensively material — cities rebuilt, vineyards planted, gardens cultivated — resisting any purely spiritualized reading of eschatological hope. The return from exile is both historical (the post-exilic return) and typological of the ultimate restoration under Messiah.

verses.flow: "And I will plant them on their land, and they shall never again be uprooted from their land that I have given them," says YHWH your God.
verses.note: THEOLOGICAL: The final verse grounds Israel's permanent restoration in YHWH's own giving of the land — the land is His to give and His promise to preserve. The phrase "never again be uprooted" (lo yinnashu od) is the most absolute promise in the chapter, functioning as the eschatological seal on all prior restoration promises. TYPOLOGY: Read canonically, this "never again" points beyond the post-exilic return (which was partial and temporary) to the Messianic Kingdom, in which the renewed people of God — including believing Gentiles incorporated through the raised booth of David — inherit the new creation (Rev 21-22).
